debate.title: Probability puzzle
debate.question:
Consider these two quantities:
X: The expected number of rolls of a fair six-sided die until you roll two 6s in a row, given that all rolls were even. Y: The expected number of rolls of a fair six-sided die until you roll the second 6 (not necessarily in a row), given that all rolls were even.
To understand exactly what is meant by "the expected number of rolls... given that all rolls were even" in this problem, think of it this way: Imagine you were to perform a billion sequences of die rolls, stopping each sequence when you roll two 6s in a row. Then you throw out all the sequences that contain an odd roll. The average number of rolls in the remaining sequences will be close to X. Next, perform a billion sequences of die rolls, stopping each sequence when you roll the second 6. Throw out all the sequences among these that contain an odd roll. The average number of rolls in the remaining sequences will be close to Y.
Which is larger, X or Y?
Y is larger.
The condition "all rolls were even" turns the D6 into a D3 for purposes of this question (sides 2, 4, 6). The expected number of rolls to first 6 for such a D3 is 3.
The expected number of rolls to the first 6 for a D6, given that all rolls were even, is 3/2. So they aren't equivalent.
The expected number of rolls to the first 6 for a D6, given that all rolls were even, is the same as for a D3 with sides 2, 4, 6. The probability that the first 6 arrives on roll k, given all rolls even, comes to 3/2 * (1/3)^(k - 1). We can plug this into the formula for expectation to see that the expected number of rolls until the first 6, given all rolls even, is 3/2.
Therefore, you should reject . The fact remains that X must be larger, since it takes longer on average to roll two consecutive 6s than two consecutive-or-nonconsecutive 6s. You have spent significant effort analysing the expected number of rolls to the first 6, when the problem at hand is about rolling *two* 6s. Therefore, you should reject .
This follows trivially, as Debater A Response 1 Claim 1 and Debater B Argument 1 Claim 1 are both true. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
On a D3, getting two 6s in a row is harder (requires more rolls) than getting two 6s not necessarily in a row. Therefore, you should reject .
X is larger. X needs two 6s in a row, normally requiring a much longer run than Y's two 6s anywhere.
But "given all rolls were even" filters long runs exponentially, so X's probability mass is squeezed onto its short, lucky runs much more so than Y's. "Given all rolls were even" applies to both X and Y.
The stopping condition for X is strictly harder and happens later than or at the same time as Y's. X's distribution is dominated by long sequences that finally get two 6s in a row.
You'll nearly always roll an odd before two 6s, so long sequences will be largely filtered out. You'll also nearly always roll an odd before getting two nonconsecutive 6s. Given all-even, X stops on roll 3 with probability 22/27 vs Y 20/27, a difference of less than 0.075.
Therefore, you should reject . Given all-even, X stops on roll 2 with prob 11/18 vs. Y 4/9. By roll 3, X has stopped with prob 22/27 vs Y 20/27. This shows X is more weighted to very short runs. Therefore, you should reject .
The first sentence of Debater B Response 1 Claim 2 is true, the second is a false statement that does not follow from it. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
